([/tɛmpiː/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*tem-PEE*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key");) is a [suburb](/wiki/Suburb "Suburb") in the [Inner West](/wiki/Inner_West "Inner West")[[3]](#cite_note-3) of [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney"), in the state of [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales"), [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"). Tempe is located 9 kilometres south of the [Sydney central business district](/wiki/Sydney_central_business_district "Sydney central business district") in the [local government area](/wiki/Local_government_in_Australia "Local government in Australia") of [Inner West Council](/wiki/Inner_West_Council "Inner West Council").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/Wolli_Creek_Tempe_House.JPG/250px-Wolli_Creek_Tempe_House.JPG)](/wiki/File:Wolli_Creek_Tempe_House.JPG)

*Tempe House*

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cd/%281%29Lymerston-1.jpg/250px-%281%29Lymerston-1.jpg)](/wiki/File:(1)Lymerston-1.jpg)

*Lymerston*, built in 1843

**Tempe** ([/tɛmpiː/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*tem-PEE*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key");) is a [suburb](/wiki/Suburb "Suburb") in the [Inner West](/wiki/Inner_West "Inner West")[[3]](#cite_note-3) of [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney"), in the state of [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales"), [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"). Tempe is located 9 kilometres south of the [Sydney central business district](/wiki/Sydney_central_business_district "Sydney central business district") in the [local government area](/wiki/Local_government_in_Australia "Local government in Australia") of [Inner West Council](/wiki/Inner_West_Council "Inner West Council").

Tempe sits on the northern bank of the [Cooks River](/wiki/Cooks_River "Cooks River") and is separated from [Sydney Airport](/wiki/Sydney_Airport "Sydney Airport") by the [Alexandra Canal](/wiki/Alexandra_Canal,_Australia "Alexandra Canal, Australia"), also known as Sheas Creek. The [Wolli Creek](/wiki/Wolli_Creek "Wolli Creek") waterway also empties into the Cooks River.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tempe,_New_South_Wales&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Tempe was named after the mansion on the southern banks of the [Cooks River](/wiki/Cooks_River "Cooks River") in the area that is now known as [Wolli Creek](/wiki/Wolli_Creek,_New_South_Wales "Wolli Creek, New South Wales"). [Alexander Brodie Spark](/wiki/Alexander_Brodie_Spark "Alexander Brodie Spark") (1792–1856), an immigrant from [Elgin](/wiki/Elgin,_Moray "Elgin, Moray"), [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland"), built Tempe House in 1836. It was named after the '[Vale of Tempe](/wiki/Vale_of_Tempe "Vale of Tempe")', a beautiful valley set at the foot of [Mount Olympus](/wiki/Mount_Olympus "Mount Olympus") in [Greece](/wiki/Greece "Greece"), which was prominent in ancient Greek legend. Tempe House, designed by [John Verge](/wiki/John_Verge "John Verge") (1772–1861) in the [Georgian](/wiki/Georgian_architecture "Georgian architecture") style, is regarded as one of the great houses of Sydney. It is listed with the Heritage Council of New South Wales[[4]](#cite_note-4) as well as the State Heritage Register.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Tempe House and surrounding buildings operated as a [Magdalene asylum](/wiki/Magdalene_asylum "Magdalene asylum") from 1885 to 1985.[[6]](#cite_note-6) Spark also donated money towards the purchase of land and the building of [St Peter's Church of England](/wiki/St_Peters_Church,_St_Peters "St Peters Church, St Peters"), which gave its name to the suburb of [St Peters](/wiki/St_Peters,_New_South_Wales "St Peters, New South Wales"), to the north of Tempe.

Frederick Wright Unwin, who gave his name to Unwins Bridge Road, was prominent in the legal profession in Sydney's early days. [William Bede Dalley](/wiki/William_Bede_Dalley "William Bede Dalley"), after whom Dalley Street was named, studied under Unwin and became one of the members of the first parliament in New South Wales in 1856. A prominent businessman during the late 19th century, William Fanning, had Fanning Street named after him, which is situated in what used to be known as Tempe Village.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

Way Street was named after the Way family. The solicitor Richard Henry Way built *Lymerston*, an [Italianate](/wiki/Italianate "Italianate") villa that still stands in Hillcrest Street, in 1842–43. What is now Lymerston Street was originally the carriageway from the Princes Highway to the house. *Lymerston* was one of the villa estates subdivided by [Robert Campbell](/wiki/Robert_Campbell_(1769-1846) "Robert Campbell (1769-1846)"). It survives as one of the better examples of the large villas of the 1840s, few of which remain. It was a [Sisters of Mercy](/wiki/Sisters_of_Mercy "Sisters of Mercy") convent from 1915 to 1982, after which it was used as a residential education centre. It was later sold as a private home. It is heritage-listed.[[8]](#cite_note-8) There is a memorial window to the Way family in [St Peters Church, St Peters](/wiki/St_Peters_Church,_St_Peters "St Peters Church, St Peters").

Another prominent family was the Harber family. Emmanuel Harber started brickmaking in 1863. He was followed by Abel Harber, who carried on brickmaking on Unwins Bridge Road, before moving to [Marrickville](/wiki/Marrickville,_New_South_Wales "Marrickville, New South Wales"). Emmanuel, Abel and Elias Harber were aldermen on St Peters Council. There is a memorial window to the Harber family in St Peter's Church.

Gannon Street was named after the Gannon family. Michael Gannon arrived in Sydney in 1820 as a convict. After being pardoned, he opened an inn on the Cooks River Road. Fred Gannon built his [Italianate](/wiki/Italianate "Italianate") home *Hurlingham* in Union Street. It still stands and is now heritage-listed.[[9]](#cite_note-9) Jane Gannon was buried in St Peter's Church.

In 1963, [Harry Triguboff](/wiki/Harry_Triguboff "Harry Triguboff") bought a block of land in Smith Street and constructed a block of eight units. This was Triguboff's first development. Later, after his [Gladesville](/wiki/Gladesville,_New_South_Wales "Gladesville, New South Wales") development in Meriton Street, he would establish his company [Meriton Apartments](/wiki/Meriton_Apartments "Meriton Apartments").

### Trams

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tempe,_New_South_Wales&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Trams")]

Main article: [Trams in Sydney](/wiki/Trams_in_Sydney "Trams in Sydney")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/Tempe_Tramway_Depot_c.1920.jpg/250px-Tempe_Tramway_Depot_c.1920.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tempe_Tramway_Depot_c.1920.jpg)

[Tempe Depot](/wiki/Tempe_Bus_Depot "Tempe Bus Depot") from the [Princes Highway](/wiki/Princes_Highway "Princes Highway") in the 1920s

A tram service once operated from Circular Quay along the [Cooks River](/wiki/Cooks_River "Cooks River") line to Tempe. This service was extended to St Peters as a steam tramway in 1891 and then to Tempe in 1900 when electric services commenced. The line ran down City Road from [Broadway](/wiki/Broadway,_New_South_Wales "Broadway, New South Wales"), then along [King Street, Newtown](/wiki/King_Street,_Newtown "King Street, Newtown") before proceeding down the Princes Highway to its terminus at the [Cooks River](/wiki/Cooks_River "Cooks River"). At Tempe, a connection led to [Tempe Depot](/wiki/Tempe_Bus_Depot "Tempe Bus Depot"). Past the terminus at the [Cooks River](/wiki/Cooks_River "Cooks River"), a single line led over the river to the Wolli Creek Per-way Yard, a large area accessible by both trains and trams. This was where the Sydney tramways housed its maintenance material.

The cross country Cooks River - Dulwich Hill line connected the Tempe line and the Dulwich Hill line via Sydenham, operating along Railway Road and Sydenham Road in [Sydenham](/wiki/Sydenham,_New_South_Wales "Sydenham, New South Wales"). Cars for this lines got housed at Tempe Depot, one of two depots in the area, the other being Newtown. These two depots housed most of the cars used on these services, except for cars running from the City

Due to the installment of the tram line the area became a popular resort, with large crowds attracted for swimming and boating on the river. The line closed in 1957, and bus 422 parallels the former line. When buses replaced trams, the Tempe Tram Depot became the Tempe Bus Depot. The railway station opened in 1884 but was originally called Cooks River.

### Railway

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tempe,_New_South_Wales&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Railway")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/Tempe.jpg/250px-Tempe.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tempe.jpg)

Map of Contractor's Tramline

The contractors for the construction of the first 23-mile [37 km] section of the Illawarra Railway arranged with timber mills in the [Port Stephens](/wiki/Port_Stephens_(New_South_Wales) "Port Stephens (New South Wales)") and [Manning River](/wiki/Manning_River "Manning River") districts for the supply of sleepers and other timber necessary for the construction. This material was brought down by sea to [Botany Bay](/wiki/Botany_Bay "Botany Bay") in schooners and small coastal steamers. The vessels anchored off the entrance to Cooks River and discharged their cargoes on to flat-bottomed punts. Tugs towed the laden punts along the channels of the river to the head of navigation at the Tempe Dam, near the present Princes Highway bridge. On the river bank, immediately to the south of the dam, the contractors erected three wharves where the timber was unloaded and placed on small 4-wheeled railway trucks for conveyance by horse teams, over a temporary tramway,[[10]](#cite_note-10) to the company's depot which was established about midway between [St Peters](/wiki/St_Peters,_New_South_Wales "St Peters, New South Wales") and [Sydenham](/wiki/Sydenham,_New_South_Wales "Sydenham, New South Wales") railway stations. A large amount of material of all descriptions, including steelwork, for the railway bridge at [Como](/wiki/Como,_New_South_Wales "Como, New South Wales"), was also taken over the tramway from the depot to the wharves, where it was loaded on to a small paddle steamer for conveyance to Como via Botany Bay and [Georges River](/wiki/Georges_River "Georges River").

### Tempe Tip

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tempe,_New_South_Wales&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Tempe Tip")]

Tempe is the site of several large clay quarries used to supply the large brick kilns located on the Princes Highway at St Peters. These kilns supplied many of the bricks used throughout Sydney. Fossils of a very large amphibian were discovered in the clay, and one is now displayed at the reptile exhibits at Taronga Park Zoo.

The pits were used for landfill, known colloquially as 'Tempe Tip', which caught fire in 1988. The tip has been redeveloped into extended parkland. Nowadays, 'Tempe Tip' is an urban nickname for a large Salvation Army-run charity store, located in Bellevue Street Tempe; but despite the suggestion, this Salvos store is not a rubbish dump.

A comedy film was released in 2002 entitled *Tempe Tip*, ostensibly set in Tempe but actually shot in [Adelaide](/wiki/Adelaide "Adelaide").[[11]](#cite_note-11)

### Heritage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tempe,_New_South_Wales&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Heritage")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/Tempe_Pub.JPG/250px-Tempe_Pub.JPG)](/wiki/File:Tempe_Pub.JPG)

The Riverview Hotel, formerly Valve Bar & Venue, formerly the Harp Irish Pub, and originally the Riverview Hotel, Princes Highway

An historic home, formerly known as Leinster Hall and Nelson Lodge is Milford Haven. Located at 125 Unwins Bridge Road, this single-storey house, built circa 1858, is as an example of the Australian bungalow and is listed on the [Register of the National Estate](/wiki/Register_of_the_National_Estate "Register of the National Estate").[[12]](#cite_note-12)

In July 2009, Tempe residents gained Interim Heritage Orders to protect the former quarry cliff face, (the quarry where the stone for Milford Haven-Nelson Lodge was quarried from), a man-made sandstone block retaining wall built in the 1880s, a historical footpath with historical views, a late 19th-century Federation cottage and a Californian bungalow, all of which were at risk of complete demolition from a 37 townhouse development. This development was refused by Marrickville Council after over forty submissions were made against it by Tempe residents. However, this refusal was later appealed in the Land and Environment Court where an amended application for 27 townhouses was won after Marrickville Council agreed to Consent Orders with the developer after a closed door Council meeting on 20 October 2009.[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14) This subdivision, named "The Quarry", was due to be completed in September 2013.

Tempe has a number of heritage-listed sites, including:

* 44 Barden Street: [Timber Slab Cottage](/wiki/Timber_Slab_Cottage,_Tempe "Timber Slab Cottage, Tempe")[[15]](#cite_note-nswshr-1412-15)
* Illawarra railway: [Tempe railway station](/wiki/Tempe_railway_station "Tempe railway station")[[16]](#cite_note-nswshr-1266-16)
* 125 Unwins Bridge Road: [Nelson Lodge](/wiki/Nelson_Lodge "Nelson Lodge") (also known as Milford Haven)[[17]](#cite_note-nswshr-518-17)

## Population

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tempe,_New_South_Wales&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Population")]

In the 2021 Census, there were 3,550 people in Tempe. 63.1% of people were born in Australia. The next most common countries of birth were North Macedonia 3.4%, Vietnam 3.4%, England 3.3%, New Zealand 2.1% and Greece 1.9%. 67.0% of people spoke only English at home. Other languages spoken at home included Macedonian 4.4%, Vietnamese 4.0%, Cantonese 3.4%, Greek 2.9% and Mandarin 1.6%. The most common responses for religion in Tempe were No Religion 50.0%, Catholic 16.5%, Eastern Orthodox 7.9%, Anglican 4.6% and Not stated 4.5%.[[2]](#cite_note-Census2021-2)

## Commercial area

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tempe,_New_South_Wales&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Commercial area")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/Tempe_NSW_Businesses_%282019%29.jpg/250px-Tempe_NSW_Businesses_%282019%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tempe_NSW_Businesses_(2019).jpg)

Businesses on Princes Highway

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/IKEA_Tempe_2019.jpg/250px-IKEA_Tempe_2019.jpg)](/wiki/File:IKEA_Tempe_2019.jpg)

IKEA Store in Tempe

Tempe's commercial area is located along the [Princes Highway](/wiki/Princes_Highway "Princes Highway"). The main shopping strip includes the Tempe Hotel, a newsagency, a light and sound store, a doctor's surgery, a dentist's surgery, a Thai Restaurant, a picture framing business "Frame Up", and [Camera Warehouse](http://www.camera-warehouse.com.au). The Riverview Hotel, formerly Valve Bar and Venue,[[18]](#cite_note-18) formerly the Harp Irish Pub, and originally the Riverview Hotel (built in 1922), is located on the Princes Highway close to Harry's Cafe de Wheels, formerly known as Deno's Diner, formerly known as Cobb & Co., a fast food outlet designed on the 1950s drive-in model.

Commercial and light industrial premises stretch north to [Sydenham](/wiki/Sydenham,_New_South_Wales "Sydenham, New South Wales") and [St Peters](/wiki/St_Peters,_New_South_Wales "St Peters, New South Wales")–this stretch includes the largest IKEA in the Southern Hemisphere, opened in 2011. There were two small licensed clubs beside the Cooks River, the [Newtown Jets](/wiki/Newtown_Jets "Newtown Jets") Sports Club and the Cooks River Motor Boat Club. Both have now closed.

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tempe,_New_South_Wales&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Transport")]

The [Princes Highway](/wiki/Princes_Highway "Princes Highway") runs north towards the city and south towards [Arncliffe](/wiki/Arncliffe,_New_South_Wales "Arncliffe, New South Wales") and [Rockdale](/wiki/Rockdale,_New_South_Wales "Rockdale, New South Wales"). Unwins Bridge Road is the other main road in the suburb.

[Tempe railway station](/wiki/Tempe_railway_station "Tempe railway station") is serviced by the [Sydney Trains](/wiki/Sydney_Trains "Sydney Trains") [Illawarra line](/wiki/Illawarra_railway_line "Illawarra railway line") services. [Transit Systems](/wiki/Transit_Systems_NSW "Transit Systems NSW") route 422 runs along the [Princes Highway](/wiki/Princes_Highway "Princes Highway") from Tempe to [Railway Square](/wiki/Railway_Square "Railway Square"), in the Sydney CBD, while the 425 route connects Tempe to [Dulwich Hill](/wiki/Dulwich_Hill,_New_South_Wales "Dulwich Hill, New South Wales").

Sydenham railway station, on the northern border of Tempe, connects the suburb to the [Sydney Metro](/wiki/Sydney_Metro "Sydney Metro") (opened August 2024), as well as the Canterbury Bankstown and Illawarra line services of Sydney Trains.

## Worship places

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tempe,_New_South_Wales&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Worship places")]

* Tempe Uniting Church
* Al Hijrah Mosque[[19]](#cite_note-19) at 45 Station Street
* St Peter and St Paul Catholic Church (now closed)

## Sport and recreation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tempe,_New_South_Wales&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Sport and recreation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/Tempe_sport_centre.JPG/250px-Tempe_sport_centre.JPG)](/wiki/File:Tempe_sport_centre.JPG)

Robyn Webster Sports Centre

* Tempe Recreational Reserve, a large park next to Cooks River, includes an indoor sporting complex called the Robyn Webster Sports Centre, netball courts, football fields, cricket pitches and an extensive jungle-gym for children.
* Col Jones Swim centre located on Toyer Street. Facilities available are a 25-metre indoor swimming pool (catering to lap swimmers), sauna, spa and steam room. In addition, a large range of gym equipment (free weights and pin loaded) and cardio theatre containing treadmills, bikes and X trainers.[[22]](#cite_note-22)
* The Concordia German Club which was founded in 1883 is located in Mackay Park, Richardson Crescent and the Concordia German Choir meets at the Club on Wednesdays at 7:30pm for choir practice. The Concordia German Choir has annual performances at the Club in Tempe as well as at various other venues. New members to the Choir are always welcome.
